summary: I was born in Northern Wisconsin in June of 1963 and lived there for the first two years of my life. It was my earliest home before my family moved abroad. Although I don't have many memories from there, it marks the beginning of my journey.

When I was about two and a half, my family moved to the United Arab Emirates because my parents were missionaries and church leaders there. We lived in different cities within the UAE, including Al Ain and later Abu Dhabi. The UAE was a big part of my childhood and early education, shaping many of my experiences.

Al Ain was where we first settled in the UAE, and I have vivid memories of running around the mission hospital compound with camels and red sand everywhere. Later in life, I returned to Al Ain to work on a new hospital project, which influenced my decision to study South Asian and Arabic studies. Al Ain holds a special place in my heart from both my childhood and early career.

In 1967, my family moved to Jordan for one year so my parents could study Arabic at a special language school. It was a brief but important time that helped deepen our connection to the region and language. After that year, we returned to the UAE and I continued my schooling there.

I spent several years attending boarding school in Pakistan after traveling there with my mother to enroll my brothers. I returned there on my own after ninth grade and stayed until I graduated high school in 1981. Pakistan was a significant place for my education and personal growth during my teenage years.

I lived in Abu Dhabi from fourth through eighth grade and attended the American School of Abu Dhabi. I have my strongest childhood memories there, like riding my bike and feeling like it was my hometown. Growing up in Abu Dhabi sparked my love for photography and traveling.

I attended the American School of Abu Dhabi during my time living in the capital from fourth to eighth grade. It was where I felt most at home growing up and where I developed interests like photography. The school was a central part of my formative years in Abu Dhabi.

I started college at LeTourneau University in Texas studying civil engineering but transferred during my first year to the University of Wisconsin–Madison. My time at LeTourneau was brief but marked the beginning of my higher education journey. It was a stepping stone before I found my true academic interests.

I transferred to the University of Wisconsin–Madison after starting at LeTourneau University and eventually switched my major to South Asian Studies. I took a semester off to return to the UAE and work on a hospital in Al Ain, which inspired my academic focus. During my last year, I studied abroad in Varanasi, India, deepening my knowledge of Hindi and Urdu.

During a semester off from college, I went back to the UAE to work on a new hospital being built in Al Ain. This experience was pivotal because it made me realize I wanted to study Islamic, South Asian, and Arabic studies more deeply. The hospital project connected my academic interests with practical work.

After working on the hospital in Al Ain, I decided to switch my major to South Asian Studies at UW–Madison. This field allowed me to explore Islamic, South Asian, and Arabic cultures and languages, which had been part of my life growing up. It was a perfect fit for my interests and background.

In my last year of college, I studied abroad in Varanasi, India, where I focused on learning the written languages of Hindi and Urdu. This immersive experience enriched my understanding of South Asian culture and language. Varanasi was an important part of my academic and personal growth.

While studying abroad in Varanasi, I studied the written language of Hindi as part of my South Asian Studies major. Learning Hindi helped me connect more deeply with the culture and people of India. It was a key component of my academic focus on South Asian languages.

Alongside Hindi, I also studied the Urdu language during my year in Varanasi, India. Urdu is an important language in South Asia, and learning it complemented my studies in South Asian culture and history. This language study was part of my broader academic interests.

While working for a company called 2100, I attended a conference in Thailand focused on cross-cultural communication. It was there that I met Carol, a journalist from Malaysia who shared my interests in photography and culture. Our meeting in Thailand marked the beginning of a significant relationship.

I met Carol, a journalist and missionary's kid from Malaysia, at a conference in Thailand while working with 2100. We bonded over photography and cultural communication, spending about a month together and then staying in touch through letters and calls. We later reunited in the Philippines, where I proposed to her, and we married in her hometown of Kuala Lumpur.

Malaysia is Carol's home country, and after we got engaged in the Philippines, we planned and held our wedding in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia. It was meaningful to marry where her family was from. Malaysia became an important place in our early married life.

I met up with Carol again at a conference in the Philippines while working with 2100. It was during this trip that I asked her to marry me, and she said yes. The Philippines was a memorable place for us as it marked the start of our engagement.

Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, is where Carol's family is from and where we got married. It was special to have our wedding there surrounded by her family. This city holds a personal significance for us as the start of our married life.

After college and marriage, I settled in Madison, Wisconsin, where I have lived for many years. My daughters grew up here and have since moved away for college but often come back to visit. Madison has become my long-term home after many years of living abroad.
